text: This is a value projector, that being said I am giving it 4 stars primarily for it’s value, as it’s functionality and picture quality do not compare to models that are priced higher. Picture quality is decent in the dark. I use a laptop with HDMI to play movies and Netflix on it. I have also connected a chromecast ultra to it. We use it in the backyard to play movies at night. Like I said it needs to be dark so twilight will be a struggle. I think this is a good product if you are looking for something small and cheap to play movies and impress younger kids. This will not impress adults that are expecting a theatrical experience for a couple hundred dollars.

In my experience, Sabrent products are reliable, long-lasting, and reasonably priced. They always do what they're advertised to do, and this SABRENT QUICK CHARGE 3.0 54W 5-PORT FAMILY-SIZED DESKTOP USB RAPID CHARGER (Black; AX-QCS5) is no exception.

The charger’s USB ports are quite snug, but this means the cables won’t be easily loosened or dislodged. The charger ports work with most USB cables and devices; the ports are for charging only, not data transfer.

CAVEAT FOR PURCHASERS: The charger has only ONE port with Qualcomm Quick Charge 3.0 capability--it's identified by the green plastic port interior and the Qualcomm Quick Charge logo (a circle with a lightning bolt) that is printed beneath the port opening.

The advertised quick charging speeds of up to 4 times faster than standard chargers are available only to devices with built-in Qualcomm Quick Charge 3.0 capability. (For more information, or to find lists of compatible devices, try Googling “Qualcomm Quick Charge FAQs”.)

THE GOOD:
---------------
* This is a high quality 3v to 12v power supply, 1A and 12W max.
* Comes with a plethora of interchangeable DC tips.
* Awesome to have around, invaluable.

TIPS ABOUT POLARITY:
----------------------------
* Reverse polarity cable included. If you mess around with vintage stuff, this cable is important. I've noticed many of my vintage stuff from the 70's to 90's have negative inside, positive outside. Whereas most modern gadgets and adapters, including this one, have positive inside, negative outside. This cable allows you to make it so your adapter is now negative inside, positive outside. If you don't match polarity with your device, YOU WILL FRY YOUR DEVICE.

THE BAD:
------------
* Nothing. 5-Stars!

Very impressed with this tripod!! Great buy

I used this in a 2nd floor apartment. I laid it flat under my TV as I didn’t want an eye sore hanging above or even behind my TV. I wasn’t expecting good reception, but I am pleasantly surprised how good the channels come in. Good value for the cost!

Bought this cover for my Dad for the iPad mini we got him for his 82nd birthday. Obviously he LOVES his new tech toy, but he says the cover is really good and does exactly as it claims. I have the same type of cover for my iPad 3 and love it. Looks great, protects the iPad very well and is well made.

This is my daughter's third MP3 player.
She wanted to try this one, since she loved the look of the design.
We figured how bad can a player be, right?
After seeing hers, my son ordered the same player.

Well, they do play music - for a while.
They also play videos nicely, albeit very small and hard to see.

As of today, with neither one a year old yet, one has had be to be sent back
to Sansa for work (totally locked up and could not even be rebooted). They took a
month to send back a refurb, rather than the one my son sent in with his music on it.

My daughter's lasted a little longer, but does freeze up often.
However, the latest trick is that it has part of the sound going out.
She had purchased four pairs of various headphones trying to get the sound to stop
cutting out, thinking it was the headphone's problem. Nope - turns out, it is the
player. Don't know what is wrong, but one of the stereo sides cuts in and out endlessly.

She'll be shopping for a new one, but it won't be this model anymore.
They are just too cheaply made to last. The technology has been around for a long time now,
and it shouldn't be that hard to make a decent, affordable player.
However, it seems like it might just be too hard for Sansa.
I used them on my shoes  to help them stay on.

[[VIDEOID:3663a2b63cb37ea4d978a919015e9f28]] I've owned past viofo dash cams. Once I saw the A139 come out I decided to change it to this one because it had better camera quality and the inside camera.

[[VIDEOID:fc5c9137509ccd0d3c39ac7c8cb27e62]] This projector is awesome! The picture is clear and the size is compact. There are so many options with it and it is so easy to use. It even includes a really nice screen. Very lightweight. I am going to use this for Halloween, but is great for an outside movie theater at anytime! It has USB ports, HDMI, and auxiliary ports. It connects to Bluetooth and Wi-Fi easily too. Great projector for the price. The only small flaw is the remote as you have to snap on/snap off the battery compartment. If it had the sliding option, it would be better as I feel like it could break with snapping on and snapping off feature. In the video I am using the USB port with a Micro SD card with pre-loaded footage. It looks so clear!
I needed some extra memory.

Amazing picture quality - also has a 2nd to none HD quality image. No complaints on sound when on Zoom. Three light settings that are managed by touching the front of the camera. Very touch-sensitive. WELL worth the money, would recommend to others and would purchase again if needed!!

Ordered this for my son and his 17th birthday!! Chose this specifically because of the subwoofers and boy, you would’ve thought we had purchased an entire home stereo system… He was over the moon happiest with this gift- and mind you, he also received a new PS4 gaming station!!

I was apprehensive how well the sub would sound in a small Bluetooth speaker and truly, it suits the needs we have perfectly. Those needs being… “I’m 17 with a few friends over (ha! Must remember we’re in the middle of COVID-19..!!) and we want to turn up music in my room, take the music to the pool… Or, just tell people we have our very own subwoofer! Sad but true, it’s all about bragging rights at this age… If this is any indication of the coolness factor, I’ve had two parents contact me asking what exactly was purchased to make us the coolest parents of the year!! That is The best endorsement I need.
As for functionality, I’m impressed the sound quality can turn an adequate size living room into a rave, mosh pit, country jamboree… Whatever your poison is- this speaker does not distort at the higher volume, which I was concerned about. The fact the music of choice happens to be mostly rap is another story… but, that is not the speakers fault it’s my sons poor taste in music!
As you can tell I am not a stereo guru or techie nope! Not in any capacity… I do know you can enter an SD card which was pretty impressive to the kids… I know they can lay tracks if that makes anyone feel better? Personally, I just made certain that had nothing to do with using drugs- and when I was assured it clearly wasn’t… I let them do their thing!
Great idea I want to toss out… We also chose a Bluetooth microphone that runs about the same price as the speaker- And.. as the kids listen to music they can karaoke of sorts… Using a lot of fun features the microphone has (Voice distortion, echo, voice enhancing.. truly the ability to make anyone sound better than they do!) I mention this because, together these two items have created such a fun source of entertainment for the small crowd… And during the quarantine I will take it all day long!
At night- my son plugs in the speaker so it’s fully charged and they have used it for a few hours at a time without any issue a battery‘s dying… Note, if next to an outlet you could also keep speaker plugged in and use it just the same. Technically, if plugged in this could run 24/7- but, not sure you want to torture yourself that way!?
I will admit, this does remind me a little bit of my old high school days in the 80’s.. when we would carry around a “boom box” on our shoulder- yep! You never knew when you needed to have a break dancing riff-off..!! The only thing different is, this Bluetooth speaker is half the size! Looks the same but a mini me version..
If looking for something to listen to that produces decent sound quality and definitely gets a throaty base this is it! Kids will appreciate it parents be warned-
I hope this info helps.. feel free to let me know!

I bought this television (I double checked the exact model number to be positive that t I am reviewing the correct one) used at an estate sale in January and it's been used daily in our master bedroom every since. The color and clarity of the picture is simply wonderful. In comparison to our brand new Sceptre 55" 1080p tv in the living room and the 3 year old Samsung in the guest room, this Vizio has the prettiest picture and best refresh rate. The Samsung has the best native sound though without a sound bar enhancement.

This Vizio is our only Smart Television and I have a Firestick on the living room tv. As of today, June 16, 2016, the Amazon app and others are all working just fine through the television's remote control with the direct access buttons. Connecting this tv to our wifi network did take several attempts and I had to update the firmware twice before it would connect and hold the connection for more than 48 hours. (To be honest, I have no idea if the former owner every actually used the internet connection so it may not have been updated before I got it) Since January, I have had no trouble with the connection or streaming. We do have a super fast internet service and a dual band router with this tv as the only thing typically streaming on one band.

My one star deduction is the tiny receiver area for the remote control. If you are using the actual remote rather than a universal one for the cable box, you have to aim directly at the very tiny eye in the far left bottom corner and it can take a couple of tries to hit it just right from across a big room. This drives me nuts when I'm streaming from Amazon Prime and need to pause the show quickly or try to flick through the selections. However, I would totally buy another Vizio tv again in the future if the new ones are as good as this two year old used one has been - I've definitely gotten my hundred fifty dollars worth out of this one.

So you spend $140 on a Fitbit. If you decide to buy one of these damn things then buy the extended warranty. Mine died within the first 90 days because I actually sweated using it and the place where it plugs in got sweat on it and now it won't work but guess what?!? Without the EXTENDED warranty there was NOTHING they would do so now I'm out $140 for a paperweight.

If only the battery life was better... I have the GPS turned off and I only measure my blood oxygen level when I'm asleep yet it is nearly impossible for the watch to last a little more than 24 hours.

My next Garmin will be the one with a solar powered assist.

That being said for the negatives this watch has the features I wanted at a good price:

- Sleep quality/quantity
- Blood oxygen level
- Steps counter
- Flight of stairs counting
- Water intake recording ) manual input)
- Multiple activities recording (it can detect when I'm running, bike riding, etc.)

It has been durable and the screen is still scratch free.

If poor battery life isn't a deal breaker buy it.

[[VIDEOID:9b88dadfbf58bb503f1928922845b685]] I ordered this dash camera after stumbling across a dash-cam video on the internet & realized I could potentially save myself a lot of hassle either in an accident or even a police encounter. I had one a few years ago when I had a long commute but these days I do most of my driving in rental cars & in unfamiliar areas I wanted something small, portable & quick to install. This camera just takes a second to stick to the windshield with the suction mount & it takes a mini USB plug for power. It comes with a 12V port adapter with a very long cord but I mostly power it with a short USB cord & my own adapter.
I'm very pleased with the video quality & sound. I have tested it with 1080p30 1020x1080 & 720P60 1280x720. Given the long record time with the included 32gb memory I will probably keep it at 1080p. The microphone picks up the sound of the turn signal switch which is good & also my singing to the radio which is bad.
I don't know how well the video resolution will come across in the attached video but I'm impressed with the image I get on my computer, especially files made at night. In reviewing the videos taken at 1080p (& with my filthy windshields), I have to be right behind car to make out its license plate. I have since maxed out the resolution to 1440P because of this but I'm not sure it made that much difference. The wide angle also tends to make things look further away than they are, the car in the last clip was much closer to my car than it looks in the video.
The included 32gb memory card holds over 4 hours of video at 1080p30 1020x1080 resolution & that's day & night. The camera will over-write files that aren't saved & it has a "save" button that stores files where they won't be over-written. The software also has an adjustable feature that automatically saves files where an impact is detected. In the 2 cars I have used it, a big pothole or bump in the road will trigger an incident on the medium setting (see my video). The camera will also take still a still picture when the impact detection is triggered or the file is selected for saving.
The camera doesn't have it's own battery but so far it has managed to save the last file if the car shuts off the 12v port or usb when the key goes off. I have mine to record short, 5 minute clips as a precaution against this, The clip length is adjustable up to 10 minutes. Without the battery, won't record any activity when the car is parked even if the car is bumped if the cord is not plugged into an active port.
Great little cover.

These are some pretty good in-ear headphones. They work only with iPhone so make sure that is what you are buying them for. On the cable they have a multi controller that allows you to control your phone, answer calls, end calls, repeat calls, has a microphone built in, play, pause and advance in the track. It also allows you to adjust the volume when you play your music. I wouldn't even bother with headphones anymore that don't provide these extras.

The sound quality of these is pretty good. They also came with some extra rubber covers so you can find the one that makes them fit best in your ears. The price is right for these too, so all in all a pretty good deal.

Having recorded, mixed and mastered music for several years, true sound is crucial. These headphones are very inexpensive and you get more than your money's worth. The sound is uncolored by any kind of equalization and the clarity is impressive.
Highly recommended for project studio engineers or simply for everyday audio monitoring.

I've owned///used this keyboard since the iPad Air 2 was introduced. It wasn't inexpensve. As I'm typing this review I'm having to fight with one of the more annoying issues - repeating keys. And gods forbid you should hit the delete key to get rid of some of the repeating characters because the delete key will repeat and delete what you've just typed. The rubber pads on both sides of the keyboard mysteriously started peeling off not too long ago. They don't seem to be absolutely necessary, so I stopped trying to stick them back on and instead, peeled them off and cleaned the glue residue they left behind. Battery life is waning - I have to charge the case almost nightly or the orange light starts flashing and it loses connectivity or shuts down. Other than those issues, it still works. It looks good and fits well. I haven't had any magnet issues and the backlight is a godsend.

** update ** It's only fair to also point out that Belkin must monitor Amazon for comments about their products. I didn't contact them - they proactively contacted me and arranged a replacement for my QODE case/keyboard. The unit is two years old, and given my experience with most other manufacturers, I wasn't expecting anything, so this is a nice surprise. Kudos to Belkin customer support for what I consider to be service above and beyond. I've received the replacement and have been using it for a couple of weeks - no sign of the connectivity or repeating key issues and (of course) the rubber bumpers are intact.

These binoculars are all about quality in the largest field of view. If you want to see a wide expanse of nature or the heavens downrange from your position, these are the way to go. I got them specifically to frame smaller constellations and asterisms, the detail of which can be further examined with a scope. They help tremendously to put the object in the scope into context. Ordinarily, observing half a degree of field tends to abstract objects almost entirely from their surrounding. Since it's not practical to really "zoom" in using a single optical instrument, using these in combination with something of higher power is ideal. Don't get me wrong, I do have a zoom on the scope... it zooms from 25x to 50x -- amazing but you have to understand that this is a field of view of 2.4 to 1.5 degrees -- essentially like looking through a pipe to a drinkstraw. These Nikons however, show over 9 degrees field of view -- something which is unsurpassed in the highest-end binocular out there.

There are compromises necessitated by the optical principles used to make these function. They do not have a very flat field of view, meaning objects in the center of the field (circle) can be focused, but objects nearer the edge of the field are out of focus. This is because the focused field is curved not flat. This problem is also evident in most 10x50's for example. It's less of a problem in good quality 8x42's like Nikon's Monarch for example, and obviously the high end Swarovski, Leica, Zeiss etc. This problem matters more when you have a narrow field and highly magnified image where the subject tends to fill the field of view. It matters less when the subject is focused in the center of the field and the background surrounding it is slightly out of focus.

Still, even if you pay the biggest bucks for bright, flat, and sharp images from the top of the line binoculars, you'll still only see about 8 degrees true field of view, whereas these give you over 9 degrees. Furthermore, they give an apparent field of view of over 59 degrees, rivaling the top-of-the-line binoculars.

If you're looking for a handy, all-purpose binocular, the roof-prism 8x42's are proven to be far more popular for a number of good reasons (more compact, more magnification while not yet needing a tripod or IS like 10x does, more competition and choices etc.) But if you're looking for the best field of view with high quality optics, this is it, and fortunately it comes at a very reasonable cost. I don't know what other 7x35's are out there, but I can tell you this is far better than Celestron or Bushnell.

I ordered the 43 Inch/110 Centimeter Light Reflector. It comes in it's own little zippered bag. The fabric appears to mostly be nylon, so it should wash easily with a damp cloth. The stitching is solid. I didn't know what to expect, but the Light Reflectors are exactly as pictured. My only little gripe is I had to refer to the listing to remember how to fold it to fit back into the pouch. Once you remove it, you have a large disk, silver on one side, black on the other. This has another zipper on the side. Inside the reflector's zipper is a diffuser which you will need to remove from the larger pouch to use. This is where the mounting tab is located. You will need the mounted clip that is available as part of the kit, or you may purchase it separately. That's not all. The large, outer pouch is reversible, so you then reverse the large pouch and you have a gold reflector one one side or a solid white on the opposite side. This is how you have black, white, gold, silver and a diffuser all in one. The diffuser has it's own tap to clip on your tripod, which pokes through from a button hole to mount the entire piece. You will need to make sure it's pulled through if you remove the outer layer of the reflector.

This is a good purchase for the value. I'm a hobbyist, and sometimes take photos of my projects. My problem comes from being a night-shift worker. I live and work at night. I must make my own lighting setup if I want to photograph anything at night, even in my home. It's light weight, and folds to a small circle, so this is also portable. It weighs less than a pair of jeans.

Here's a free tip: Only use the gold if your subject is brown. I don't find the gold reflector so useful. I'm glad it's available, but I doubt it will get much use for me. Also, place the reflector in a way that would mimic the sun's light, and your subject will look more natural. This is where the clips and extension arms from your tripod can come in handy if you are working solo. Of course, you can always enlist the assistance of another for outdoor shoots. Sometimes this is a best option if it's available. In the movies, they tend to show someone holding a reflector with it facing up towards the subject, but that's only because it does look silly holding a big disc over your head. If you hold it, you will need to hold it up over your head, and it will look silly but your photos will not.

It's a good tool to have on hand for any level of photography. I mostly photograph my doll collection. Once or twice a year, our doll collectors group will gather outdoors for shoots. We've not had a reflector available to us before, so this will help us immensely finding good spots to make photographs, even if the clouds are moving or we want to setup under trees.

I also ordered the 2-pack clips. These are nice because they come with an adapter on them. If you remove the threaded black female cylinder and threaded ring, you are left with a standard sized male mount that will fit a tripod base. You do not need to make a separate purchase for an adapter, it's already there. The ball joint swivels all the way around and there is a groove in the base to tilt the clip in towards the base, so you can use these clips for holding the reflectors, diffuser or even a backdrop on a side or top mount adapter of your backdrop frame.

I have used and tested more Ethernet cables than I can remember and there is no question, all Ethernet cables are not created equally.

Typically any device requiring an Ethernet cable will come with one in the packaging - it will work (usually). A good comparison is those cables are much like an old car - it should get you there, whereas this cable is like a high end sports car - it will get you there lightning fast, without failure.

This Cat8 is thicker than some due to the shielding within and additional insulation. This cable is made to withstand and holdup to exterior environments for the long term. The connector bases are reinforced at each end of the wire which really help hold all of the wires in place even if you have to bend and twist it to fit it within the plug. The connector plug itself is gold plated which is the ultimate standard in connectivity.

This Cat8 Ethernet Cable is 30’ long which makes it absolutely ideal for using it to directly plug your TV or gaming system into your router. So many of us stream programs and movies via our TV and there is no comparison in speed between a WiFi connection and a wired connection. For gamers, it is absolutely the way to go - do away with all lag caused by your WiFi by connecting directly to your router.

You will not find a better exterior grade Cat8 Ethernet Cable - this is really a top of the line cable that you’ll love.

The last Blu-Ray player I owned bit the dust about a month after we got it and I have been quietly looking for another one to replace it. I was given the opportunity to try this one and I took it. It's not a bad player and it seems to work, but there are a couple of behaviors about it that I do not like.

It is as simple a player as it gets. It's not fancy and it doesn't do a lot of extras beyond basic player functions - pause, play, eject, skip, etc. At the end of the day it's all that's needed. It connects via HDMI which is fine, and the resolution and picture size can be tweaked in the menu system which is good. Playback is fine and I have had no issues with picture quality.

There are two things about this player that bother me, both of which are annoyances but neither are deal breakers. First, when the unit is powered on it asks for the user to set the language and voice prompting. That's fine the first time. But, this player does it every time it is powered on again and that gets irritating. Second, when a disc is put in and playback starts, there's a very brief hesitation in the sound within the first 10 seconds of playback. This hesitation is just enough to notice, and then it's gone, almost like it skips a quarter-second of sound or something. It doesn't happen again until an eject operation and a disc is inserted again. I have observed this with multiple discs.

There is no HDMI cable in the box. Keep that in mind when you buy - you'll need to either have a cable already or you'll have to buy one separately.

This is not a bad player and so far it is working better than our previous Blu-Ray player. It sticks to the basics and it gets them right. There are a couple of minor odd behaviors that bug me a little but at the asking price I can overlook those.

I like this magnetic mount a lot. Not only are the magnets very strong but the metal base plate is also heavy enough to put the camera at an angle without it tipping over even when it isn't on metal. I've been using it in my shop to record projects & it easily slaps on to my tool boxes, cars or motorcycle tanks & frames & holds well enough to adjust the camera. The it comes with a nice, aluminum thumb tightener. The mount is all metal so it takes a bit of force to squeeze the metal plates to the plastic adapters. The kit also comes with a wrench to give some more leverage on the tightener but I haven't needed it.

I really like the "ZeroTouch" voice-command app that comes with this ZEROTOUCH DASHBOARD CELL PHONE HOLDER (With Voice-Command App for Android Phones). It was easy to set up on my Galaxy S7 Edge, and it seems to work very well "on the road".

I haven't tested it a whole lot (because of physical installation issues, more on that later), but it seems to have an excellent, up-to-date GPS Navigator, and a terrific Text Message Handler. To send or reply to a text, you wave a hand to wake the app, tell it to "Send a message to (name a contact)", dictate the message, listen to the readback, and answer "yes" to the query "Should I send it". If you have the Samsung "S Voice", it's quite similar, except that "ZeroTouch" notifies you of incoming text messages, and lets you reply to them strictly with voice commands.

The "ZeroTouch" app only works when your cellphone is docked with the cellphone car mount base. Docking and mounting are where I ran into problems. First, there aren't many places in a Honda Civic that are suitable for a suction-cup-base cellphone holder--at least not if you worry about the suction cup losing its grip and dumping your expensive smartphone on the car floor.

Second, the cellphone docks with the car mount base via a thin, adhesive-backed magnetic plate that you're supposed to attach directly to your cellphone or cellphone case. (Tip: If you're having trouble pairing during setup, place the thin metal plate that attaches to your phone directly atop the round part of the base until the magnet grips.) Attaching the plate was an issue, because I didn't want to apply it directly to my new phone (and maybe mar the finish). Applying the plate to my cellphone case worked for a short time, but the adhesive wasn't strong enough to hold the cellphone case through repeated dockings and undockings. Now the adhesive is worn out and won't adhere to anything.

However, there is a workaround. If only the magnetic plate is docked on the base, the "Zero Touch" app will work whenever your cellphone is NEAR the base--within Bluetooth range, I guess. So my hands-free calling/messaging arrangement uses the Honda Civic's two cupholders. The base with the docking strip goes in one cupholder, and the cellphone with the "Zero Touch" app goes in the other.

With this setup, I can use all of the "Zero Touch" features. Of course, if I want to use the handy "parking location finder" feature, I need to undock the magnetic strip from the base when I leave the car, just as I would if the strip were still attached to my cellphone.

The magnetic plate did NOT interfere with my S7 Edge's wireless charging feature, when I had the plate attached to the lower quarter of the cellphone case. (The "ZeroTouch" app setup has a diagram showing you where to attach the plate, if you use wireless charging.)

Because I love the easy-to-use "ZeroTouch" app, but am dissatisfied with the holder mounting arrangement (suction cup and adhesive may not adequately secure the cellphone), I rate this product at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).

They do not leak sound, like some other headsets I have used, when the volume is loud. This feature allows the user to play music and not disturb others while listening in public.

Packaging: While some may feel that these earphones should be stored in a hard case, they are not, so keep that in mind if you are worried about protection. These Sennheisers fold up which makes them convenient for travel and storage. They fit into a small draw string storage bag with a detachable cable. The detachable cable allows the user to control the volume, pause, forward and play songs. The cable also allows the user to stop and answer phone calls. The clarity is very clear. I have an android phone, but I am sure that they are compatable with the Iphone as well.

Sound Quality: These headsets have a frequency response of 18000- 22000 hz which allows the listener to hear those deep bass lows and high notes that are popular today in a large genre of music. Again, the sound quality is very good and great clarity.
Overall, these headphones are 5 out of 5.

[[VIDEOID:f0109ee47644b763ae68b64d78805cb6]] I ordered the 'RGB LED Video Light for Camera Camcorder,Hagibis King 10 Rechargeable Pocket Size 360° Full Color Light with 2500-8500K Color Range,9 Lighting Effect Mode CRI/TLCI≥97 (Full Color)' for review and would like to share my thoughts.
I have a variety of Pocket lights and this one tops the charts for compact size, brightness and features. It has three modes to choose from. The first is the basic White light mode where you can adjust the temperature from cool 8500K to warm 2500K. From this mode you can also adjust the brightness. The second mode is the HSI or as I call it the RGB mode. This mode allows you to change the color of the light. From this mode you can also adjust the saturation of the chosen color as well as adjust the brightness level. The third mode is the effects mode which has 9 different pre-programmed flashing light modes. From this mode you can also adjust the brightness level of the effect. The third mode I rarely use, but it is a fun one to have and I will eventually use it for videos.
I love the HSI or RGB inlay meter that is on the back of the light. This gives you the HSI reference number for specific colors you may want to use. I also love, and very much appreciate the 1 inch OLED display on the back that allows you to see and choose your settings. Other pocket lights I have do not have this feature and it makes it a pain to try and figure out what setting, battery level or color mode or temp that you have set. The pre-mounted support bracket is also a nice bonus to this pocket light. It rotates 360 degrees which makes adjusting your angle quite easy. The kit also includes a USB C cable for fast charging, a cold shoe mount and a soft cinch up bag to prevent the light from scratches while tucked away in your camera bag or backpack. The body of the device is made of premium Aluminum Alloy which makes for a very solid build quality.
I use this mostly for photography and I love how compact it is and how bright it is. Overall for the price, this little guy is a powerhouse and I think it is an invaluable addition to your camera bag.

I use this to adapt my [[ASIN:B0047T79VS Belkin F8Z492TTP Bluetooth Music Receiver]] to the input jack on my [[ASIN:B0036B8A20 Kustom The Defender 5H Class A Guitar Amp Head]]. This is a power-saving solution I discovered for listening to music through the amp in my music room versus firing up my power-hungry stereo system. I then play music via my bluetooth-enabled phone. It's a kludgy solution that keeps my power bill down. It also shows how this adapter can work in one of an almost limitless number of situations.

What I most like about this adapter is it is designed around a block with no dangling wires like, for example, a [[ASIN:B000068O4W Hosa YPR102 TRS Male To Dual RCA Female Y Cable]]. The key advantage is this adapter will not break at the weakest point - the wires. In the past I have had the wires on the Hosa-style adapters go bad or even break due to frequent handling. Every time you insert male RCA jacks into a Hosa-style adapter you are putting stress on the wires, which will eventually break down internally. In some cases the result is a noisy connection with intermittent static, but in others the signal is lost due to an internal break in the cable.

The design of this adapter has none of the above mentioned points of failure. However, it does have one disadvantage that the Hosa-style does not: it will not fit into cramped places. Since I am plugging mine directly into the front of an amplifier the design does not matter. If you were to use it as an adapter to an amp or mixer that connects at the rear, you may run into space problems. Also, my amp connection does not have space constraints to the left or right, so, again, the design is perfect for me. For connections that have knobs, or other components close to the 1/4" connection, you may find that only a Hosa-style adapter will meet your needs.

One final thing: while the design allows for stereo signal input at the RCA connection end, this adapter sums those signals into mono. That means that your stereo source will not be heard in stereo with this particular adapter. The Hosa model mentioned above will maintain stereo separation of the RCA signals, so if you want to maintain stereo, the Hosa is the way to go.

I own the [[ASIN:B00MVXUACM Koss SP330 On Ear Dynamic Headphones Black with Silver Accents]] (current price $105.52) and I love them because of their comfort, good sound, and overall quality. I selected these headphones because I wanted to have a spare set and also to see how these would compare to my Koss.

I tried these headphones on the iPhone 5, iPad 3, and a Dell Latitude E6430 laptop and on all of these devices the sound was fabulous. It was actually more stereo sounding than my Koss.

PROS:

- The stereo sound is crisp and slightly better than my Koss.

- I LOVE, LOVE, LOVE the electric blue color which is called "candy blueberry."

- A headphone port on each earpiece provides the user with the ability to share the headphones by plugging in a music share cable which is NOT PROVIDED. A music share cable is not really needed in my opinion because all headphones come with one cable. While my boyfriend was listening to music on his iPad, I just plugged in my Koss cable to the side of the headset he was not using, and listened to the same music through my Koss headphones while he was using these.

- I like that the provided headphone cable can be used in the left or right earpiece which enables me to switch sides when I want.

- The headphone cable has an in-line MIC control for Android, tablets and other devices. Based on my research, an in-line MIC means there's a built in microphone that is built into the cable which should enable the wearer to answer calls without removing the headphones. I was able to get this to work on my boyfriend's iPad and iPhone and he was able to talk and hear through the headphones. There is a one button control on the in-line MIC which lowered the volume.

- The headphone band is adjustable.

CONS:

- The only CON I see is that they are not as comfortable for me as I would have liked. I used these a few times for a period of several hours. Even though they can be adjusted, I wound up getting a headache with extended use (more than 2 hours). So in this sense they were not as comfortable as they initially feel when just putting them on. The Koss headphones I own are the only ones I've ever tried that have been comfortable on me.

OTHER THINGS TO NOTE:

- The Amazon product page says that a music share cable is included; this is not correct nor is it really needed because if you are sharing just plug in a cable from another pair of headphones to the extra port on these.

- According to the manual, it takes 20 hours for the headphones to break in. It says the sound mellows out over time.

In summary, these headphones are great. They have fantastic stereo sound (at least to my ears), the ability to share by plugging in another set of headphones, and an in-line MIC. They are adjustable and relatively comfortable but not as comfortable as my Koss. The color is also attractive.

***The first part of this review is for the Red option***

I needed more than just a simple case for my Kindle as it pulls double duty serving as my tablet and my planner. I also needed something that would provide it with supreme protection as it accompanies me to and from work every day.

I purchased this case/portfolio in the red color in hopes that it would make it stand out at work. Many of my co-workers have Kindles as well and I didn't want mine to accidentally get picked up. The red is nice, but way too much for me. I have been considering exchanging it for the black and the following issues are what sent me over the edge and led to my returning the red case.

This case/portfolio worked nicely for my needs. However, I removed two stars for two reasons.
#1 The case stunk to high heaven. It was so bad that I thought a stink bug had gotten into my house. Though the "scent" did lessen over a few weeks, it was still very present after five weeks.
#2 The description claims that this case has the ability to put the Kindle to sleep when you close it up. Not true. After my battery died over night, I realized that the sleep/wake feature was not working in my case. I had to physically turn my Kindle off before closing the case (may not seem like much of a hassle, but it quickly becomes a time consuming annoyance).

There was one benefit to this case though. It is the first case I have had for my Kindle that does not have any issues with using the Camera App while still encased in the removable inner sleeve. This was a very nice feature, however not nice enough to get me to keep it.

**This is the review of the Black option**

So, I just returned the red case and opted to give the black version a shot. It arrived this evening and wow, what a difference!

It doesn't stink at all and the sleep/wake feature is functioning properly! I do, however, now have the ghastly on-screen message of needing to slide my Kindle up in its case to take a picture. But this does not bother me very much as I have not used my Kindle for taking pictures more than a handful of times in the month that I have owned it.

All in all, it is a nice, professional case/portfolio that offers the features for my Kindle that I was needing. It also has a fair amount of pockets inside, though do not expect to be able to store much bulk as you will run into two problems..
#1 If you add too many things to the pockets, you run the risk of the pockets bulging and possibly damaging your Kindle's screen.
#2 Also, if you add too much to the pockets, you will possibly not be able to close the case. And if you manage to close it, you may run the risk of stretching the zipper and ultimately damaging the case.

Personally, I keep a small stack (about 10) of my business cards, five gift certificates and a little cash in the pockets and the zippers still flow smoothly with no issues and nothing from the pocket side comes in contact with my screen. Overall, I would recommend this case, as long as you are willing to take a risk on getting one that has the features you are wanting/needing as it seems to be a completely random toss-up as to which case has which features.

On a side note, the included stylus/pen is a great idea, but it isn't executed very well here. The plastic tip leaves a lot to be desired - it takes more force than I care to apply to my screen for the Kindle to register the stylus' contact on the screen. I did some research and purchased [[ASIN:B00E0GV9IC The Friendly Swede Bundle of 6 Micro-Knit Hybrid Fiber Tip Universal Capacitive Stylus Pens 4.5" for Smart Phone Touch Pad + 6 x 15'' Elastic Tether Lanyards + Cleaning Cloth in Retail Packaging]]. These stylus' are VERY nice in appearance and easy to use without requiring much force on the screen at all.

Purchased this system because, at the time, it was the only way to obtain a 1080 Ti next day, but received a fabulous overall system which far exceeded my expectations.

For background, I usually prefer to build my own computers, but would have incurred a ~$500 penalty if I took that path this time because of the ridiculously inflated 1080 Ti prices.

Per the attached photo, my system is benchmarking at the 99th percentile for CPU (holding stable overclocked to 4.9+ ghz using the automated bios tool), and 99th percentile for GPU (slight factory overclocked MSI Aero, but haven’t tweaked further yet).

It’s a bit funny that the system only scored 98th percentile in the 2d benchmark. My guess is that the test isn’t taxing enough to bump the GPU out of power saver mode (some of the compute 2d tests later on run at over 1600 FPS). Note that with regards to these benchmarks, the system hardware was tested “as is” apart from the CPU overclock.

My only concern is not terribly substantial, because I had already planned to replace the HD before I placed the order on Amazon. The HD is only benchmarking at the 69th percentile. It’s actually benchmarking pretty far below average for the WD blue 250 gb ssd, but I’m not overly concerned because it will soon be replaced. You shouldn’t be concerned either because you’re buying this system for the 1080 Ti, not the starter hd.

The keyboard and mouse are acceptable starters. I’ll be replacing these soon as well (as almost everyone buying a $2000 system will be) but they were actually better quality than I expected. Phrased another way, I’m prioritizing the new SSD over new keyboard and mouse.

Overall build quality is good - the system runs fairly quiet (though not as quiet as an iMac, but to be expected with this kind of gpu power), and looks beautiful. It’s a bit time consuming to open the case, but one does so infrequently to the extent it’s a non-concern.

In terms of gaming performance, this system is overkill for playing anything at 1080p - you should expect performance close to 150-200 FPS at 1080p.

Have been playing on a 4K gsync monitor. Witcher 3 and AC Origins performance has typically been 40-45+ fps, with all settings maxed. Sometimes it will run surprisingly close to 55-60fps. Suffice it to say 4K ultra is a stunning experience for a (former) console peasant like me.

In summary, highly recommended. Definitely choose this system if considering versus an off the shelf model. You’ll probably receive this system before a competitor starts building an alternative box. Note, this system has increased in price since my purchase, but it’s not unexpected given current ram and GPU availability constraints.

I bought and installed the Pogoplug on my LAN for the purpose of sharing photos over the Internet.

Right out of the box I had misgivings. There is no documentation or technical specifications. That immediately raised serious questions in my mind about it being vulnerable to hacking or opening access to my computer and personal information. You have to attach it to your modem/router and allow it to go out on the Internet without knowing what it is doing. I decided to take it on faith that the company was legitimate and not going to install a backdoor into my system.

I attached a Seagate USB external hard drive as my main memory device. I have also used USB memory sticks for temporary storage. All are USB 2.0.

Initially I had issues with people accessing my files. The Web access used by outsiders does not show any indication of activity. If you have a couple of hundred photos like me, it can take a couple of minutes for anything to appear. I have also had people report 404 and other errors fairly frequently. My workaround was to create more folders to compartmentalize everything. They load quickly enough for people to see something happening and know that they are connected.

Once you get inside the folders, people see a filmstrip and a large image like they would see in Windows. It is a serviceable but not glamorous interface. It is also not customizable.

Once I established that people could access my photos over the Internet, the next step was to fill up the hard drive. To do so, my plan was to push the files over my 10/100 MBps LAN. The Pogoplug shows up as a drive, so all I have to do is drag-and-drop. The problem is that it is incredibly slow. I normally get about 30 kBps. That is dial-up modem slow.

This exposed the second big problem. Remember the total lack of documentation? I had no FAQ, troubleshooting guide, technical specifications or other information to go on. I had to contact technical support via the Pogoplug web site. The Web site is slow and not always working, but I did get to someone, and they gave me the obvious suggestions like turning everything off and back on, dismounting and remounting the drive, trying a different drive, etc. After several iterations, I still had a slow connection that was now unreliable, a bunch of tech talk gibberish on ports and processes to open and close, and a closed out support ticket.

Doing more research, this appears to be a known and frequent problem. It is a deal breaker for me. I am going to be looking for a replacement. As an interim solution, I will attach the USB HD directly and transfer my files on an as-needed basis. However, I am looking for an alternative now.

So, to summarize, the concept is great, the implementation is okay, but the tech support and the LAN connection are not good. If you are serious about file sharing, this is not the device for you.

In order to try the over burn feature, I would need the appropriate disks that can normally hold 8.5 GB (DVD+R).  These disks are not too difficult to find online, but the office supply stores only seem to stock 4.7 GB disks.  I guess you either have to special order or buy online.  Most everything that I need to burn can either fit on a 4.7 GB DVD or can be compressed (without much loss of quality) to fit.  It may be false economy to buy the higher capacity disks, as they are nearly double the price of lower capacity.  With burnable media prices being so low anyway, you don't gain much by squeezing as much as you can on a particular disk.  So why did I get this drive?  I like to have a drive on the shelf if one goes bad (and they do sometimes), and then I don't have to wait for one to arrive.  I assumed it was probably good to have one that can burn to maximum capacity as this isn't much more expensive than a regular DVD burner.  Since I haven't actually installed or used the drive yet (waiting for disks), one should not pay as much attention to my review as some other reviewers.  I just wanted people to be aware that you can't insert a 4.7 GB disk and expect to over burn to 8.7 GB.  You need to understand the differences in available media and get the appropriate blank media. Update:  I finally received the appropriate media to test the drive.  It seemed to work good for the first 6 or 8 disks, but then 3 disks in a row had burn errors.  Bad media?  I don't think so; it appears the drive is failing already.  I'll test on another computer and see if it is the media or the drive and update the review again, but right now I can't recommend this drive at all.  UPDATE 2:  I tried this again after rebooting my computer and it now seems to work fine and does indeed overburn, so you can squeeze a lot on one disk.  When I had 3 bad burns in a row, I thought it was the burner, but it may have been bad media.  On the other hand, I suspect something flaky on my PC, as I have had bad burns before occasionally (not recently) and then after a reboot everything was fine again.  I may have too many programs loaded when I try to burn.  That's not the fault of the DVD drive.  Most likely the fault of the burning program (and I am using a major well-know program.)

I have had several water resistant shower speakers and none, not one, my $600 Samsung Galaxy phone isn't. . . nothing is waterPROOF. It's a lie. Many advertisers and sellers like to claim waterproof status but what they mean is water RESISTANT. IPX7 means a device can survive immersion, up to 1 meter (3 ft 3 in) depth for up to 30 minutes. Let's also be clear here; those tests were with pure, likely purified, filtered water not highly chlorinated pool water. Not salty sea water. Not brackish lake/pond/river water. And also not water containing soaps, shampoos and conditioners. All that. . . stuff in every day water is going to reduce the effectiveness of the water resistance of any device.
So, I unbox this and it's light. Lighter than just about any speaker I've used. I look at the specs. . . 3 watts. THREE watts of power? Okay it's supposed to be primarily used in a small enclosed indoor space but it also says it's great for camping, hiking and general outdoors stuff (please, don't blast music when you're hiking, wear headphones, show some respect and restraint). I've never seen a non-novelty speaker that was less than 5 watts. It feels light and cheap and thin. The suction cups; they send two likely expecting one to fail. Neither worked. The best I could get with the cup wet with soapy water, alone, no speaker, stuck to a mirror for maybe five minutes. I can't get these suction cups to stick to my desk. No worries, my shower has a shelf. I turned off my oontz Angle 3 and connected this to my phone using Samsung Music as a player to take advantage of the Dolby Atmos and excellent sound (only PowerAmp sounded better of the half dozen players I tested and PowerAmp is a paid app only) quality it offers. I guess if you wanted noise in the shower this would work. Sound quality was about as bad as you'd expect. Distorted bass, harsh highs, muddy midrange. No EQ settings I tried made this sound good. It's just not worth it. You can for just a little more money get far better sounding water resistant speakers. You should enjoy the music in your shower or bath not suffer with it.

I upgraded from a year-old, previous-generation Paperwhite, mostly to gain the new inverted white-on-black screen mode that Amazon insists requires new hardware and to have built-in Audible playback over Bluetooth.

This unit ticks most of boxes, and after some debate I've decided to give it five stars, but there are some imperfections worth knowing about. It's mostly good, though, and often great.

Battery life
No complaints. In several long international flights, I've yet to run it down more than about 30%, either in long reading sessions with the front-light or in lengthy Audible playback sessions over Bluetooth.

Audible content
The Audible player works brilliantly. Excellent sound, simple controls (including playback-speed adjustment). It does work best if you don't have a screen password enabled, though-- that will get in the way if you just want to rewind a few seconds to catch something you missed. Playback proceeds even if you have a case closed, which shuts off the front-light and saves battery charge.

There's no headphone jack-- this is for Bluetooth only. Fine with me. I have Bluetooth-enabled hearing aids (cochlear implants, actually) and it's a true joy to be able to listen to books on this device. Thanks, Amazon.

Screen
The screen is pretty much the same as my previous-generation Paperwhite's, meaning not bad but could use more contrast when reading in a dark room using the front-light. Outdoors or in well-lit areas, it's fantastic and mostly glare-free.

More seriously, the lack of contrast mars the new Paperwhite's valuable ability to invert text to white-on-black. This is a marvelous accessibility feature for those with "floaters", vitreous separation and other vision issues. Unfortunately, when used with the front-light in a darkened room, such as when reading at night or in a darkened airplane, the black background is more of a medium gray. So this new feature is less helpful than it could be. In fairness, poor contrast is characteristic of today's e-ink displays, which provide perhaps 8:1 contrast. If Amazon can significantly improve the contrast, I'd upgrade again in a flash.

The screen is plastic and will scratch. Keep it clean and use a case. Even with a case (I prefer one of the various "origami" cases available here, which allow the Kindle to prop itself upright) it will fit nicely into a pocket.

The touch-screen user interface is the same as the previous-gen Paperwhite's. I'm not a huge fan of it; too easy to touch it when you don't mean to, and end up on the wrong page. But, the earlier button-driven Kindles had sort of the same problem, so whatever. I recommend a simple fix, perhaps as a Settings option: require a double-tap rather than a single-tap. How about it, Amazon?

Connectivity
The 4G/LTE capability is a nice speed upgrade from the previous 3G connectivity. Downloading Audible content requires a WiFi connection as before.

Unfortunately I'm finding that global connectivity with the new 4G hardware is inferior to the previous-gen's 3G radio. For example, my year-old 3G Paperwhite connected without issue in Saigon, but the new one won't.

The user interface for turning Airplane Mode on and off could be improved. As it is, engaging Airplane Mode cuts all connectivity including Bluetooth. So if you're in the middle of listening to a book when the time comes to put your gizmos in Airplane Mode, it will cut your connection. Better to do what the latest iPhones do, and have the device learn if your usage prefers that Bluetooth remain active.

Browser
It's high time for Amazon to replace the minimally-functional "Experimental Browser" with something more useful. I get it that Kindles are all about battery life and browsers can be major energy hogs, but c'mon, it's 2019, and this browser feels like a relic from 1996. Probably is.

While I have tested and reviewed Bluetooth speakers costing ten times (and more) what this does, the sound quality from this speaker is really quite good. In relation to the sound quality I would place them on par with mid to high end Bluetooth speakers.

In regards to charging time and battery life, both are outstanding. This is an all around great Bluetooth speaker that I certainly recommend.

Stephen M. Charme's incredibly helpful June 19, 2014 review covers this device in exhaustive detail. I'll attempt to not repeat him, so will confine my review to why I awarded my review sample five stars.

Foremost, I love the convenience of having an all-in-one power and charging station for my phone, tablet and a few smaller electronic items. It takes up one full two outlet wall connectors in my music studio where I am using it. One of the plugs is a dummy that does not draw electricity. This ensures that you do not overload a circuit as well as providing stability to the rather large unit.

At 15 Amps (1675 Watts) this is more than sufficient for the small amperage devices I am using (although I do have a 5 Watt RMS output tube amp plugged into it as well.) Where I live the power fluctuates too wildly to plug anything of value directly into a wall outlet, so this is essential.

It's also a convenience with respect to the swivel. Most of the devices I am powering with this use wall warts, and even with the swivel I can only plug four in at any given time. While that blocks two of the outlets, had it not swiveled I would have probably lost one more outlet.

I have found this so useful that I intend to order another to place in my gig bag for playing out. There is always a dearth of outlets on most of the small venue stages where I play, and the power in most of those places is usually abysmal. One of these for a small PA and keyboard will afford adequate protection as well as being conveniently small.

This mouse is fine, but nothing special. The small USB transceiver is nice. It's out of the way and won't catch on anything. The mouse itself tracks on just about anything and is very reliable. The battery compartment is easy to access, no hassle replacing the single battery. I comes with a brand name battery uninstalled which is very nice. No software to install these days, the old Vista machine recognizes it without issue.

However, it's too lightweight for my tastes. The “click” feels cheap to me and the scroll wheel is stiff. I have an older Logitech wireless mouse that has the larger USB transceiver and the batteries are a hassle to change, but it feels solid and works great. To be fair, this is a “Mobile Mouse”, so maybe lightweight should have been expected. I just didn't realize how much I would dislike the weight difference.

I won't send this Microsoft mouse back, but I wouldn't buy it again. I haven't had any problems with this mouse, it's just personal preference. I'll just use the Microsoft as a travel mouse. I suppose it's also possible that operation becomes smoother with use, but I'm in no hurry to use this mouse.

I just started using my camera yesterday and my first impression is that this thing seems very fragile.  Hope I don't drop it, as it might not survive the fall.  It is extremely small, light and compact.  Think of two packs of cigarettes or two packs of playing cards.  It is that small.  Great for travel due to the light weight and compactness.  Got it for about $90 on sale; would hate to pay more than that for it. Very easy to operate, but if you have big hands, this will be too small for you.  Be warned that it won't take more than a couple minutes of video without a memory card, so you'll have to put a little more money into it if you really want to do video or really much of anything.  The first video I took came out pretty decent, but it was in good light.  I tried some still shots inside at night and they came out very dark in spite of the flash.  The flash doesn't seem adequate, so this is probably a camera best used outdoors or in the daytime.  Great camera for a kid.  This is a good camera for small hands and a great size for kids.  If they break it or lose it, you aren't out a bundle.  As another reviewer pointed out, it does only take video in the ASF format; not MPEG, as the advertising suggests.  I think MPEG might be just music files.  I didn't need to install the software to capture the pictures or movies on my computer (Windows XP).  Another great thing is that this is a complete package.  It has the camera, a carrying strap, a carrying case, USB cable, TV cables, headphones, battery, battery charger and software.  It does not come with a lens cap and the cap is not available either.  You can buy an optional tripod for it and with the small size, I think that could be useful in some situations to help hold and stabilize the camera.  I am not sure how you store music on it; I probably won't use that feature, but it seems like it would be a nice compact music player; again you'll need a memory card to save much of anything on it.  One problem I had so far.  The memory card (which you have to buy separately--a standard SD card) slips in the bottom under a little rubber membrane.  The first time I held the camera I accidentally popped out the card and lost the shots I was trying to take.  This might be a problem and I am thinking I may have to put a piece of heavy tape over the bottom to hold the membrane and memory chip in place and prevent ejecting the memory again.  Another downside that I see is the battery.  You can't just pick up some spare batteries at the corner store and pop them in.  You have to use the special rechargeable battery.  If you don't have a spare one charged up, your camera will go dead and you'll have to wait until you can plug in the charger and recharge the battery.  That's another $20 for a spare battery.  My digital camera uses standard AA batteries, so I can use either rechargeables or regular batteries.  If this camera used regular batteries, it would make it larger and heavier, so the small rechargeable does help on the weight and size.  Don't buy this if you are a serious photographer.  Buy it for occasional use, for easy portability and as a first camera to learn on.  Controls are easy and straightforward.  Nice camera for the money, but not for serious photographers.  It does a lot for a tiny little thing; sure doesn't make me want to go back to the days of huge video cameras that you had to hold on your shoulder, due to the weight.  Minaturization can go too far and this is about as small as I would ever want to go; might be easier to use if it was just a tiny bit bigger.

I am primarily a stills shooter. That said, I do have an excellent camera that is very capable of shooting video and I do need it for such on occasion. I was interested in this camera slider as a way to get smooth shots and to reduce reliance on lens-based stabilization which is not included in my camera body. This has been a learning experience and while I like this slider there is definitely room for improvement.

I will start with the basics. This is a large and fairly heavy animal, which is to be expected. It is roughly 3' long and in it’s most basic configuration rests on a flat surface with no issues. It does have some adjustments that can be made to the individual feet on the frame so it can be adjusted slightly for uneven but mostly level surfaces without using any other equipment. To get much more versatility out of it, though, it can be mounted on tripods on either end to raise it higher or to set up angular shots.

The motor on the frame is controlled by a remote which comes with a battery pack. The remote is not wireless and has to be connected to the motor via the provided cabling. This is all right, but wireless functionality would make it much more versatile. It should be noted that the remote controls the slider, not the camera. I had some concern about this in the very beginning, thinking that the remote would be incompatible with my camera and that this unit would not work for me.

I encountered some issues that bother me. First, when powered on and running the motor runs the slider down the track until it is either stopped or a stop point is set. If one is not set, it bumps into the end and keeps trying to go beyond, making an awful noise that will surely mess up any video that requires sound. In my mind, the motor should detect when it hits the end and stop, even if start and stop points are not set.

When the motor is running and it is not near a stop point, it runs at constant speed and it is very smooth. When manually paused, it stops quickly but it doesn’t jerk to an abrupt stop like it hit something. But, if one of the end points are set and is reached, the motor slows down, then jerks to a stop, enough to notice in video footage. This is most obvious when the remote is set up to film in a loop as the issue can be observed repeatedly.

The motor is not silent, though it is quiet. It is not loud enough to ruin a shot unless absolute quiet is needed, and the motor produces a low-volume, low-frequency hum that slightly resembles the sound of an electric trolling motor on a bass boat, but quieter. The point is that it is by no means silent, but it should be good enough in the majority of cases. This is out the window if the motor is allowed to reach the end of the track before it is stopped, in which case the noise is likely to be a serious issue.

This is a decent slider and if used with some thought will serve nicely. There are definitely some places where it could be improved, particularly in the way it fails to handle reaching the end of tracks.

I was really looking forward to trying this tablet out. To date I'd had no exposure to Windows 8.1 or a dedicated Windows tablet. I have had a decent 10.1 inch Android tablet.

First are foremost, the elephant in the room... or rather, the mouse. This is a seven inch wide screen tablet. I have friends whose who online lives are conducted via five inch cell phone screens and they're happy with it. The screen on this tablet is nice. Don't get me wrong. I was able to stream from Amazon video in HD (720) and the video was crisp and clean. Images display well but, again, the screen is just seven inches. I can't imagine trying to be productive with something so small.

For a device with only one gig of RAM this runs Windows fairly well. I wws able to load several Windows programs including the Calibrě eBook manager which took a little longer to convert eBooks between formats but did the job. I also loaded a legacy email and Usenet program that I've been using for a decade or longer. Along with directly installed programs I also downloaded a handful of apps from the very buggy Microsoft app store. The store worked less than 50% of the time while I was trying this tablet out. I at one point had to resort to switching to a local login account and then back to my Hotmail logi. It only took about an hour of reading web forums to find that solution to the Windows app store refusing to work.

Two problems I ran into were the sound which is from a single speaker hidden in a slot on one of the short sides of the tablet. If you want to listen to anything other than the default Window's beeps and tones you'll want to pair this with a blue tooth speaker or headphones. The speaker in this tablet is deplorable. The next problem I had was the tablet runs HOT. I was running a program designed for Windows 98, downloading and reading posts un Usenet and viewing a few posted vacation and astronomy images and I had to stop. The tablet was so hot it was uncomfortable to hold and I worried it might overheat and become damaged.

While I was impressed to see Windows running on a small tablet I was thoroughly unimpressed with Windows 8.1 the tile system is just terrible.

So, it has good video quality on a too small screen with horrible sound and a flaky app store to rely upon, though you can download and install normal Windows programs. I just wasn't that crazy about this tablet. And will definitely be sticking with Android and a much larger screen.

Projectors for home use are typically set up for home theater systems. I have seen a number of systems set up with projectors suspended from the ceiling, and I have seen a few that rely on being mounted on a table top. This is a table top model and it is a very good and easy-to-use projector.

This is a 300-lumen projector which is fine for home theater systems, though it would be a little underpowered to use in a larger room or something like a small church. It is good for small presentations too and is a good option if you want to take it with you for meetings instead of keeping it permanently placed somewhere.

There are several input sources for which the projector is configured. For testing and experimentation I found it easiest to work with HDMI though I was able to use some of the other sources as well. It accepts VGA (which is great for older computer systems and other hardware), it can read files from an SD card or even a USB drive. Using a USB drive or SD card, this projector becomes very compact and portable and doesn't even need an external source of input.

This projector even has a pair of speakers inside so you can use this projector for the entire A/V experience, though you will get better sound from a dedicated sound system. This projector is capable of receiving sound as well as outputting it.

I used the HDMI input to feed the image from my computer to the projector and it did a fine job of displaying my desktop on the wall for me. I was also able to configure Windows to use it as an extended display so I could then look at PowerPoint on the computer screen and use the projector to output the slide show. This is very, very convenient and makes this configuration ideal for presentations. I was able to adjust the focus manually and get the image as sharp as I wanted too.

The remote with this unit is great. It provides full access to the projector's functions, so if the projector is on a shelf out of reach the remote is sufficient for any adjustments you may want to make. If you prefer not to use the remote or have some reason you would rather directly interact with the projector there are several buttons on the top of the projector that give access to the different functions. You can change the input source, power on and off and get into the menu system which allows you to tweak all manner of settings: brightness, change the resolution, update the firmware from USB, set the date and time and more.

This projector does output a lot of heat – to be expected from a projector with a bright light in it. Make sure that you have decent ventilation in the room when using this projector unless you want the room to warm up. I did notice that when the projector is shut off, the fan stops too which is a minor disappointment – I would rather have had the fan continue for a few minutes afterward to cool down the LED.

This is a nice projector. It accepts a lot of different input types, it works well for small rooms and home theaters and it is easy to take with you due to the small size. It is especially useful if you want to run your video or presentation from either USB or from the SD card. If you're looking for a small-scale projector, this is a good choice.

My old HDMI extender required a power adapter on both the tx and rx side, but this can actually send power over the network cable (PoE) so that the other side does not need its own adapter. Now THAT's awesome!
This is one of the primary reasons I was interested in this, as there are two times I use such an adapter: When I use my projector instead of my TV for movies, and when moving a camera around at an event that I want to send a live feed back to a screen or streaming box for. Currently I have to supply power at both ends of the network cable, but I imagined ONLY needing power on the receiver, and having THAT power the transmitter. For example, in the case of the camera, I could have the screen receiving the signal send power to the transmitter by the camera using PoE and be somewhat more free of tethered power on that side. Also, when using the projector, I could turn on the outlet controlling the projector via remote and have it power on the transmitter behind the TV as well. Less wires and outlet spaces where already difficult / congested is good! THE PROBLEM, however, is that this thing misses its potential by only sending power from the TRANSMITTER! In other words, I can send power from the tx with PoE to the rx side, but the RECEIVER can't power the transmitter! It only goes the same direction as the HDMI signal. This, to me, was disappointing as that's the side I wanted to save from having more wires to plug in. Not the receiving side. That side is never an issue to supply power.
So I was a little upset about that. But otherwise, it is a solid product that seems to do what it claims to, and so far seems more reliable than my previous setup. If it could send power using EITHER side, it would be perfect! So close this time!

When listening to music, a good sound system can make a huge difference and it is known fact that the built in speakers on an iMac, while good, are not the greatest at producing a high quality level of sound. This is where the Tango Bar can make a difference. It attaches quickly to your iMac, creating a sound experience that is far better than before.

First, let's look at the appearance. This speaker is nothing more than a rectangular bar and is thus very utilitarian. It isn't going to win any awards for style because it is very ordinary looks- wise. But with its aluminum construction, it does match the appearance of an iMac and thus, when hooked up, it looks right at home.

The Tango Bar includes an auxiliary jack for input, a connector for a subwoofer, and a headphone jack. The sound quality from the device is actually quite good even if a little lacking in bass. The sound is similar in quality to a low- wattage boom box with the bass turned low. There isn't a huge volume of sound with this speaker, but it should be fine for its intended use.

Audio quality can make all the difference when listening to music or other sounds from your iMac and the Tango Bar does offer a noticeable improvement compared to the sound from the built in iMac speakers. Its metal construction means the speaker is made to last and while it could use a few improvements, it is still a good option for iMac users who want to enhance the audio experience.

I'm a burgeoning audiophile and I'm always eager to try new cans to add to my listening experience. A while back, say 2007 or so, I jumped into the deeper waters and decided to upgrade my stock Apple ear buds (why I didn't sooner is beyond me). I haven't looked back since, and continue to look forward this very day.

But do these headphones do my ears and music justice?

Right now my arsenal consists of some Sound Blasters I use for my computer and my trusty ol' Sennheisers which I've had since 2007 (the selfsame headphones with which I used to help me jump from Apple), along with Monoprice ear buds I keep at work. I'm not the most professional audiophile, but I sure as heck can tell some differences in quality, especially if the sound source is in FLAC.
Unfortunately, I'm not the most ecstatic about SMS Audio's STREET by 50, especially at the higher premium for Star Wars branding.

I've always been wary of celebrity-endorsed products; never trying Beats, I've been bombarded by them on the streets of Chicago by the candy-colored headbands on people's heads. I've always wondered about their integrity, until a fellow flesh-and-blood audiophile renounced them after she had used her ATH -M50s for some time. I took her word for it and never considered using Beats.
But me, being the Star Wars fanatic, had to try the SMS Audio. When they arrived I admit I was impressed by the fancy packaging. For $200, the headphones arrived looking like it.
They came in a slipcased box that was a little difficult to remove as the vacuum-sealed enclosure proved a tough fit, but they open up sweetly after that.

There are some nice manuals that come with, along with some neat little Star Wars stickers and papers. There's even a cleaning cloth and nice soft zippered case. The headphones came folded inside the case.
When I opened 'em up and took out the included removable cable and tried them on, I was a bit dumbfounded and how uncomfortable they felt around my head with such soft ear pads. I'm not sure if I need to break them in longer, but I've been using them steadily for a month and so far I haven't noticed a difference. My ears ache after just 20 minutes.

The sound was very disappointing at first, though I admit it did warm up (or down?) after a few weeks. But are they a large improvement over my $30 Sennheisers? A little, but not considerable. I can hear more background noise--that is, in the music--but there's not much balance as I have to turn up the volume to notice any huge improvement. There's a bit more bass than I'd like, but not enough to drown my ears in it.

I like the fact that the cable is detachable, but the ones they included feel a bit cheap and they tangle easily. I HATE the fact that they have the buttons, something which I've always hated since the iPhone came standard with them. But that they are removable altogether makes up for the buttons, as I'm able to change them out with custom, and higher quality, gold-plated cables.

The colors are fantastic; honestly, they were the reason I was drawn to them, but they look metallic in the press shots whereas, when you get them, the material is actually plastic, forcing the colors to be a lot lighter, more matte, and cheaper in feel. I'm all for matte, but I had hoped the headphone band was metallic; alas, it's plastic.

So that's my take on the cans. Take them or leave them. They're nice headphones, and I've gotten plenty of complements on them both from friends and at work, but at the steep price tag I can't recommend them, especially when you can get far superior headphones for much less (like the revered Audio-Technicas).

We have a minivan with a DVD entertainment system pre-installed. It predates the HDMI standard, so there are just RCA analog inputs in the back of the van. Since DVDs are pretty much obsolete, using this adapter we are able to plug in a laptop, raspberry pi or other digital video player to the car's system. I like that it is powered by USB, it is a convenient source of power especially when you don't have access to outlets (like in a car!). But you have to be careful about ground loop feedback. When powering it off a laptop, and the laptop is charging in the car, it can create either an audible hum and/or visual static distortion pattern on the video feed. This is common in DC circuits that share ground when working with analog equipment, unfortunately. I'm not sure if there's much that can be done about it other than try to run off batteries as often as possible (with this converter attached to a USB battery bank, the laptop on its own power, and the car running its own 12v DC). I have an older similar product looks almost identical that does the same thing so I'm guessing that's just a side effect of working with RCA inputs instead of digital ones.
