I have used and tested more Ethernet cables than I can remember and there is no question, all Ethernet cables are not created equally.

Typically any device requiring an Ethernet cable will come with one in the packaging - it will work (usually). A good comparison is those cables are much like an old car - it should get you there, whereas this cable is like a high end sports car - it will get you there lightning fast, without failure.

This Cat8 is thicker than some due to the shielding within and additional insulation. This cable is made to withstand and holdup to exterior environments for the long term. The connector bases are reinforced at each end of the wire which really help hold all of the wires in place even if you have to bend and twist it to fit it within the plug. The connector plug itself is gold plated which is the ultimate standard in connectivity.

This Cat8 Ethernet Cable is 75’ long which makes it absolutely ideal for using it to directly plug your TV or gaming system into your router - even if located in a different room. So many of us stream programs and movies via our TV and there is no comparison in speed between a WiFi connection and a wired connection. For gamers, it is absolutely the way to go - do away with all lag caused by your WiFi by connecting directly to your router.

You will not find a better exterior grade Cat8 Ethernet Cable - this is really a top of the line cable that you’ll love.

This is a purchase that you’ll never regret.

First off, let's be clear that this isn't really a hi-fi speaker. With that out of the way, this is a very excellent speaker.

Pros
- Compact size, weighted well enough so that it won't scoot around on a table
- Very pleasing sound, even if it's not hi-fi
- Plays pretty loud for its size
- Small, throw it in a suitcase or travel bag easily -- ideal for use in a hotel room
- Speakerphone function is excellent
- Looks beautiful, build quality excellent
- Easy to pair with my phone, quickly restored when turning on
- Includes 3.5mm jack for connection to devices that don't use Bluetooth
- Sparse but logical controls
- Recharges with standard cell phone charger, charges quickly, and lasts plenty long on one charge (especially if you keep the volume down)

Cons
- I didn't hear any bass with this. I can be happy enough without bass at this price point.
- And the high treble is missing too. (However, the resulting sound is clear and pleasing.)
- It's not stereo. That was probably obvious from the shape of it. But just sayin'

I've found that placing the unit next to a wall makes the sound a bit fuller, even though there's still not any sign of what I would call bass. But the sound is very well engineered, so the sound that you end up with is exceptionally pleasing and clear.

I've also tried using this in the car, and it's good. My car is from the pre-Bluetooth era, so there's no way to hook up the phone to the stereo. I can listen to my tunes in the car with this. The sound doesn't rival the excellent sound from the car stereo, but it works just fine. I'm very happy with this.

for me this tripod represents the right kind of compromise. i do art as a part-time hobby. i like nice optics, and everything else really just needs to be good enough. this tripod does not radiate the aura of high-end engineering, manufacturing, and materials; it simply conveys good sense. this tripod is easy to use, easy to transport, and easy to throw in a dumpster if it got run over - i.e. i have no psychological attachment to it because of preciousness/expense like i do with nice lenses or overpriced/cutting edge cameras. this tripod is like a good pair of shoes that feel great and can go anywhere

3.5 STARS. I rounded up because it is more good then bad:
Bought as a Christmas gift for the boyfriend. We are long distance and we FaceTime a lot. Well, actually, I FaceTime with the underside of his kitchen cabinets, the blanket on his bed, the ceilings in all his rooms, or the top of his nightstand. He talks away, oblivious until I say something. It's funny mostly but sometimes in a relationship you need all the connection you can get, even if it's their face on your device while they smile or laugh or talk you through a bad moment. So, I got him this for Christmas so that he could hands-free me and I could still see his pretty face. He finally put it together and started using it the other day... with an asterisk. *THE INCLUDED SCREW/BOLT IS GARBAGE. It was machined badly. Really badly. So it only screws maybe halfway into the arm/neck or whatever it is called, which sits in the weighted base. Thankfully he really doesn't need the screw/bolt because the arm/neck (like a metal dowel, I suppose, that inserts into the base maybe half an inch. The screw/bolt is inserted from under the base and - if it fits correctly) recesses into the base when it's tightened. As this one had threads that weren't smooth about halfway down on the screw/bolt, it left the screw/bolt jutted out. The base won't sit on the nightstand or other flat surface with this issue unless he just doesn't use the screw/bolt. I'd post a photo for clarification but the stand isn't available and getting him to send photos isn't always something I remember when we do talk.

The screw/bolt is I'm sure metric and it does use an Allen wrench (included) to screw in; the threading is very tight, so finding a true replacement screw/bolt is probably gonna require going to a specialty nuts/bolts/hardware store. That's annoying enough to take off 1.5 stars for me.

Overall I think these are decent walkies. If you want basic push button to talk and small lightweight with adequate range. This is a decent choice. Choosing this model for the Secret Service earpiece can be hit and miss. Guess some earpieces can be just as bad as the black over the ear units the other companies use. In all but a library, the earpiece audio is too low to hear in outdoor conditions. ***see update***

The RT22 cost the same as other models with displays and buttons (thicker and heavier walkies) to control functions this model has to do with button combos, but it does talk to you as you change settings and channels. Sadly as with many other models I tried. VOX is near useless. VOX works as bad as any sub $100 walkie I've tried. It lags and maybe sends half the message at best. You won't know how good/bad VOX is for you till you try it yourself.

Get these for size, lightweight, basic, rechargeable walkie needs. Try to catch it on sale...if possible. I paid a fair amount.

Btw, my wife and I use these for trail rides on our e-bikes. The true two-way communication devices did not fit our non-helmet style and will cost substantially more.

***UPDATED***
Company did reach out to offer a refund because they thought I was unhappy. And only the earpiece was a bit of let down for me. They told me about another similar earpiece that seems to be working a bit better and now I can actually hear outdoors with the earpiece. It is still a Secret Service style, but somehow it is a bit louder. Maybe my original earpieces were faulty.

Let me be clear. I still paid the retail price, kept the walkies and did not take any money/refund for my review. The walkie has so far been a great trail ride walkie. I might have received a bad earpiece. The company seems to care about their ratings and customers use of their products. And we all need to keep what we buy if it works to avoid landfill waste. I just came back to add a star and let others know. Good walkie. And maybe consider one of Retevis's many other earpieces or handset mics. Some of their other walkies include better headsets.

For the price, this camera works pretty darn well. My router/office is at the end of the house furthest from the front on the first floor, and I wanted a camera on the second floor up front to view my front lawn for when people come and I don't hear the bell and for the damn annoying neighbors that mess with my things (watching to see who is going to mess with my garbage cans as I write this)... and it is working GREAT! The refresh rate at 640x480 is far bettern than I had expected.

The picture looks pretty good but while I can see individual leaves on trees, I can understand why one would have a hard time recognizing someone's face unless you knew them, not the clearest/sharpest picture, but certainly good enough to see what is going on.

It is also true that as soon as it starts to get dark, and I mean just starts to, the camera becomes useless. I have also not found any other way of viewing the camera's output than through the ActiveX component (but I haven't tried too hard yet either).

However configuring the camera was very easy. You connect it using the wired network port (you can connect this camera wired or wireless, which I think is something they fail to advertise enough), you go to the 192.something.something.x site of the camera, tell it to use DHCP, change it's IP address, set up your wireless setting and WAP, and you are set. You can unplug the camera from the network and power (it retains the settings) and you are set.

I have it hanging upside down from the ceiling of my window using a magnet. Pretty light.

So if you are looking to recognize people's faces from across the street, this isn't for you. If you just want to see that there are people across the street, then it is great. I got more than I expected. Well worth the money. Guess it is all in what you expect ;)

The New VanTrue T2 with larger screen than the N2 plus 1080p. My first impressions are that the new larger screen is a nice improvement from the N2's smaller screen. That being said, if you're not a fan of larger screens, this may not be for you. For me I like to be able to see and adjust the camera position and the smaller N2 screen was quite difficult. Quality of the T2 is just as good as the N2 which I am quite impressed with. My only complaint so far is that my new unit came with a small scratch on the lens which I will post a picture of here. At first I thought it was a speck of dried glue. However after trying to clean it off with rubbing alcohol and a Q-tip, I realized that it was in fact a scratch which is the ONLY reason I am not giving it 5 stars. After taking it for a drive to get some video footage from the Dash Cam, I did not notice any effect from the scratch so that was good. Setup was all pretty easy and very similar the the N2. The new T2 does come with the "Always on power adapter" which was a surprise that I wasn't expecting. I will eventually install this but for the time being, I am just using the cigarette lighter cord that came with it and plugging that into an "always on" cigarette lighter socket in my Van which so far seems to work just fine for the "Parking Mode" option.

Though again the T2 does NOT come with the GPS mount, they do include a card with instructions on how to possibly WIN one for FREE!!

Would I recommend this Dash Cam? Absolutely! Great company to work with and very active on social media.

My son wanted a computer for school work on his bedroom desk, but there isn't a lot of free space there. A wired mouse and keyboard looked very cluttered very quickly, so we looked for decent wireless options. I found some affordable Chinese ones, but my son wanted the ability to game every now and then and the cheap ones had reviews that claimed connection would sometimes die in the middle of use and require reconnect.
I found this one and remembered fondly that I used to use Logitech wireless equipment when I was in college and I always had a good experience with them. This model is no different, it handles his school work well and holds up to some light gaming without any noticeable hiccups or disconnects so far. And we can open the desk drawer and drop both the keyboard and mouse in when we are finished so he desk stays clean and uncluttered. I call this combo a win!

Well, these are certainly interesting!
I often lose my little Roku remote, with it sliding off the bed, then being hard to find under the bed, under cat stuff, etc.

These are silicone covers. If you or your child has ever bought a game controller cover, these are like this.

These are not super easy to get on to the remotes. There are so many button cutouts, it gets caught every step of the way. As a chubby woman, it brought back flashbacks of trying to put on pantyhose too soon after a shower, or on a humid summer day...good luck! 😄

But, I persisted and slowly got it on correctly. It has a textured back to help keep your grip. It does kind of cause the volume button to feel recessed a bit, but that is not a deal breaker. It also includes a wrist strap, but there is no designated punchout for it, so it seems useless to me. The strap is also too small to go over your wrist, so just a pointless addition.

I love the colors though, and it will definitely help me find it on the floor, or amid blankets. The silicone might even help stop my cat from ceremoniously shoving it off his spot by my pillow, but we'll see about thay one.

Overall, I'm pretty happy with these. The only negative side is having to peel it off again if a battery change is needed, but that isn't oftem enough to be a concern.

These are a really great set of Walkie Talkies. Our family does a lot of hiking, and it helps to motivate small children to go for a hike in the woods if you give them a Walkie Talkie. The second you hand them one, they sprint ahead of you to get far enough away to start talking.

We had to do a road trip recently with two vehicles, and it was great to use these units to communicate between cars. Cell phones would have been an option, but these units broadcast without having to push an answer button. Also, if you are traveling through poor cell phone coverage areas, they work really well.

Durable and waterproof, we found that you can get a signal from several miles away. This is a great product for camping with your family and many other physical activities.

I like how this thing can be under your desk, out of sight out of mind. I tested it with my Google Pixel Slate, and everything works as advertised. For a PC without usb-c, you can use a usb to usb-c adapter, however, your monitor may not work. They give you adhesives to test out the mounting plate at your desired location, then if you like it, you screw can screw it in permanently. I think you scan skip the screws and just use it with the adhesive as is. If it ever falls off, get stronger 3m. So far it's holding up for us. From the packaging, which is shrink-wrapped, to the dock and all its accessories, everything is very professional and giftable. One annoying thing though, they put label stickers over the usb-c ports. If you're going to do that, you need to use stickers that are easily removable. These stickers were a pain to remove. After I had a fun time removing the blue sticker, I thought I learned my lesson and would go extra slow when removing the red sticker, so the layers don't separate. Even going super slow, I managed to separate the red sticker as well. LOL! Got myself some wd40 to remove the rest of the whites. We could do without these stickers folks! Just an annoyance, not enough to knock a star. I'll still rate this 5-Stars.

We have a number of gaming/entertainment devices (Roku, Wii, DVD player, etc), and this A/V switch box makes it easy to go back and forth between them. We took it one step further and used my label maker to identify each switch (just so the kids don't have to press every button before finding which one they need).

Word of warning: this box does NOT come with the A/V cords required to connect it to the TV. Of course you will already have cords for each of the devices (they typically come with them), but you need an extra set of cords for output from the box to the TV. Just a head's up. (the cords are cheap, and I think Amazon even has a "bundle" option to buy this plus cords for just a little extra)

I have owned 2 D100s, 1 Fuji/Nikon S5-Pro, a D300, D7000 and a D7100... Plus a few  F2,3,4s and some in-betweens. I chose this over the 500... I am down to taking pics of Eagles, Bears and Whales... Have been experimenting daily for about a month. Have yet to have any quality or focus issues. 100% satisfied with the features, speed, dynamic range and finally I get to use all of those beautiful glass lenses I've accumulated since my first purchase in 1972.... Almost didn't buy another DSLR with all of the awesome 1" bridge cameras out there... but each tool has it's own special use. Look forward to some studio lighting/portrait setups... Still doesn't touch the Fuji S5 on people in my humble opinion... Solidly built. Using almost exclusively with the Nikkor 200-500mm and a Sigma 50-100mm (Dx) and it is smart enough to go FX or DX based on which lens you attach... so don't hesitate if you are loaded with DX lenses. I understand the Knobs and dials... that has been a harder transition for some reviewers... Time and practice fixes that. Don't use the articulated screen on something this big.. but one day with a little lens I might. For video I use a Lumix FZ1000... Didn't buy this for video...
